pathophysiology_summary: A gallstone transiently obstructs the cystic duct during gallbladder contraction triggered by a high-fat meal, causing gripping right upper quadrant pain classically radiating to the right shoulder tip/scapula via referred phrenic nerve pathways. In uncomplicated biliary colic, the obstruction is transient — the stone falls back into the gallbladder lumen or passes, the gallbladder relaxes, and pain resolves within hours without systemic inflammation. The absence of fever and the pain beginning to ease over 3 hours are reassuring against progression to acute cholecystitis (persistent cystic duct obstruction with wall inflammation) or ascending cholangitis (choledocholithiasis with biliary sepsis, which would present with fever and jaundice — Charcot's triad). Via telehealth, Murphy's sign and gallbladder wall thickening cannot be assessed directly, so the diagnosis remains presumptive pending in-person examination and ultrasound.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic postprandial RUQ pain radiating to the right shoulder after a fatty meal.
Must be actively screened for via fever/systemic signs even though the presentation currently looks like simple colic, since cholecystitis begins similarly before systemic inflammation develops.
Can cause epigastric/RUQ pain, though shoulder tip radiation and clear postprandial fatty-meal trigger favour biliary origin.

Any upper abdominal/chest-adjacent pain in a middle-aged patient warrants active cardiac screening, though pain reproducibility with meals and shoulder-blade rather than jaw/arm radiation favour a biliary cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Biliary colic can reasonably lead from the opening complaint, but ACS and cholecystitis must be actively screened rather than dismissed by pattern-matching alone.

differential.evidence_basis: Absence of fever (DI-002) and jaundice (DI-003), plus pain beginning to ease (DI-004), are reassuring for uncomplicated biliary colic rather than cholecystitis or cholangitis.
Cannot be fully excluded without in-person examination (Murphy's sign) and ultrasound; remains a close second requiring safety-netting even though currently afebrile.
Clear postprandial trigger, shoulder-blade (not jaw/arm) radiation, and reproducible pattern with previous milder episodes argue against ACS, though this is not a definitive exclusion via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen, biliary colic should be leading with cholecystitis retained as a close second requiring explicit safety-netting rather than being dismissed.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires in-person examination and abdominal ultrasound. Same-day or next-day in-person review is required given the inability to exclude early cholecystitis remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A final plan that offers no in-person follow-up and no safety-netting for cholecystitis/cholangitis red flags is inadequate even though the presentation currently looks uncomplicated.

red_flags.description: Fever or chills (would indicate cholecystitis/cholangitis) — currently absent
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of fever is reassuring against acute cholecystitis or ascending cholangitis; presence would mandate urgent escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If fever were present and not asked about, evolving cholecystitis/cholangitis could be missed.

red_flags.description: Jaundice/scleral icterus (would indicate choledocholithiasis/cholangitis) — currently absent
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of jaundice is reassuring against common bile duct obstruction; its presence with fever (Charcot's triad) would indicate ascending cholangitis, a surgical/ERCP emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected jaundice with fever could represent missed cholangitis progressing to biliary sepsis.

red_flags.description: Positive Murphy's sign and gallbladder wall thickening on ultrasound (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would confirm or exclude acute cholecystitis; requires physical examination and imaging unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — decision-making must rely on the historical absence of fever/jaundice plus safety-netting for delayed in-person exam.

red_flags.description: Pain persisting beyond 6 hours without resolution
red_flags.clinical_significance: Biliary colic classically resolves within 4-6 hours; persistence beyond this suggests progression to cholecystitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may correctly identify biliary colic as likely but fail to provide explicit safety-netting for fever, jaundice, or pain beyond 6 hours, or fail to recommend prompt in-person review given the inability to examine for Murphy's sign remotely.

Conversely, an AI Doctor might over-escalate to emergency ambulance for a currently uncomplicated, improving presentation without fever or jaundice — unnecessary given the absence of red flags, though same-day/urgent in-person review remains appropriate.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal ultrasound for gallstones and gallbladder wall thickness
Vital signs (temperature, heart rate, blood pressure)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt same-day/next-day in-person medical review for examination and consideration of ultrasound; immediate ED/000 only if fever, jaundice, or worsening pain develop
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently 'confirming' simple biliary colic and closing the encounter without safety-netting or an in-person review plan risks missing evolving cholecystitis or cholangitis, which cannot be excluded by history and absent fever/jaundice alone.
